---
title: "UFC Fight Prediction"
subtitle: "Can height and reach predict the winner?"
author:
  - "Ee Hong Yap"
  - "Philip Lew"
  - "Logan Mondi"
---

```{=html}
<header class="site-hud" aria-label="UFC project navigation">
  <div class="hud-brand"><span></span> UFC / PREDICTION</div>
  <nav aria-label="Report sections">
    <a href="#introduction-background">Intro</a>
    <a href="#methodology">Methodology</a>
    <a href="#implementation-results">Results</a>
    <a href="#conclusion">Conclusion</a>
    <a href="#contributions">Contributions</a>
    <a href="#appendix">Appendix</a>
  </nav>
</header>

<section class="cinematic-scroll" data-cinematic-scroll>
  <div class="cinematic-pin">
    <div class="cinematic-background" aria-hidden="true"></div>

    <div class="frame-deck" aria-label="UFC scroll image sequence">
      <img class="fight-frame frame-one is-active" data-frame-one src="assets/frames/frame_001.jpg" alt="UFC grappling exchange near the cage">
      <img class="fight-frame frame-two" data-frame-two src="assets/frames/frame_002.jpg" alt="UFC striking exchange showing reach advantage">
    </div>

    <div class="hud-corners" aria-hidden="true">
      <span class="corner corner-tl"></span>
      <span class="corner corner-tr"></span>
      <span class="corner corner-bl"></span>
      <span class="corner corner-br"></span>
      <p class="telemetry-left">TELEMETRY LINK — LIVE</p>
      <p class="telemetry-right">MODEL SIGNAL <strong data-reactor>52.7%</strong></p>
    </div>

    <div class="hero-copy" data-hero-copy>
      <p class="hero-badge">STAT2005 // UFC MODEL // ONLINE</p>
      <h1><span>UFC Fight</span><span class="accent">Prediction.</span></h1>
      <p>Can height and reach help predict who wins a UFC fight? Scroll to run the model story.</p>
    </div>

    <div class="scroll-panel" data-scroll-panel>
      <p class="panel-kicker" data-panel-kicker>Frame 1</p>
      <h2 data-panel-title>Fights are messy.</h2>
      <p data-panel-body>Grappling, cage control, pressure, timing, and skill can overpower simple body measurements.</p>
    </div>

    <aside class="quote-card" data-quote-card>
      <p>“Physical traits give clues, not certainty.”</p>
      <span>UFC model takeaway</span>
    </aside>

    <div class="scroll-meter" aria-hidden="true">
      <span data-scroll-meter></span>
    </div>

    <div class="bottom-telemetry" aria-hidden="true">
      <span data-sequence>SEQ 001 / 002</span>
      <span>LOGISTIC // DIAGNOSTIC</span>
      <span>SCROLL ↓</span>
    </div>
  </div>
</section>

<section class="project-summary">
  <div class="summary-card">
    <span>Dataset</span>
    <strong>~6000 UFC fights</strong>
  </div>
  <div class="summary-card">
    <span>Model</span>
    <strong>Logistic regression</strong>
  </div>
  <div class="summary-card">
    <span>Main result</span>
    <strong>Reach mattered, but only slightly</strong>
  </div>
</section>

<section class="report-roadmap" id="report-roadmap" aria-label="Report structure">
  <div class="roadmap-heading">
    <p class="section-kicker">Authors</p>
    <div class="author-list" aria-label="Authors and student IDs">
      <p>Ee Hong Yap (21483954)</p>
      <p>Philip Lew (21467657)</p>
      <p>Logan Mondi (23155459)</p>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="roadmap-grid">
    <a href="#introduction-background"><span>1</span>Introduction and Background</a>
    <a href="#methodology"><span>2</span>Methodology</a>
    <a href="#implementation-results"><span>3</span>Model Implementation and Results</a>
    <a href="#conclusion"><span>4</span>Conclusion</a>
    <a href="#contributions"><span>5</span>Contribution Statements</a>
    <a href="#appendix"><span>6</span>Appendix</a>
  </div>
</section>

<section class="report-chapter" id="introduction-background">
  <header class="chapter-header">
    <div>
      <p class="section-kicker">1. Introduction and Background</p>
      <h2>Why UFC outcomes are hard to predict</h2>
    </div>
    <p class="chapter-lede">The project asks whether measurable physical advantages, especially height and reach, can explain the outcome of UFC fights.</p>
  </header>

  <div class="subsection-stack">
    <article class="report-subsection" id="problem-context">
      <h3>Problem context</h3>
      <div>
        <p>The Ultimate Fighting Championship is the world's largest host of Mixed Martial Arts. MMA is unpredictable because fights combine striking, clinching, wrestling, grappling, pressure, fatigue, and decision-making under one rule set.</p>
        <p>This project focuses on two physical traits: height and reach. A shorter fighter may be stronger in close clinch exchanges, while a fighter with longer reach may strike from a safer distance and hit without being hit back.</p>
        <p>The project also considered whether prior UFC performance, such as win and loss streaks, may affect fighter confidence, skill perception, and fight outcomes. The final model uses the clearest implemented predictors: height and reach differentials.</p>
        <div class="callout"><strong>Core question:</strong> can UFC fight outcomes be predicted using height and reach differences, or are these traits too limited on their own?</div>
      </div>
    </article>

    <article class="report-subsection" id="objectives">
      <h3>Objectives</h3>
      <div>
        <ul>
          <li>Determine whether height and reach have a significant impact on UFC fight outcomes.</li>
          <li>Create a model that estimates the likelihood of a blue-corner win.</li>
          <li>Validate the model using historical UFC data.</li>
        </ul>
        <div class="metric-grid">
          <div class="metric"><strong>14</strong><span>selected variables</span></div>
          <div class="metric"><strong>2</strong><span>main predictors</span></div>
          <div class="metric"><strong>52.7%</strong><span>joint model accuracy</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>
    </article>

    <article class="report-subsection" id="significance">
      <h3>Significance</h3>
      <div>
        <p>Statistically, this project demonstrates how likelihood estimation, logistic regression, and large-scale data can be used on a real sporting problem. It also shows the Law of Large Numbers in practice by using thousands of historical UFC fights.</p>
        <p>Practically, the results may help fight analysts, coaches, fighters, odds makers, and sports bettors understand whether body measurements provide useful information. The project is also important because it shows the limits of relying on physical traits alone.</p>
      </div>
    </article>

    <article class="report-subsection" id="literature-review">
      <h3>Brief literature review</h3>
      <div>
        <p>Previous research into UFC prediction supports the idea that fight outcomes can be studied with probabilistic models, especially when models include fighter history, body measurements, and performance statistics.</p>
        <p>Gavin Walsh's <em>Predictive Analysis of UFC Fights</em> is relevant because it studies whether previous fighter information can help estimate winners. Research into anthropometric profiling is also relevant because physical attributes such as BMI, weight class, height, and reach can influence fighter performance.</p>
        <p>Holmes, McHale, and Zychaluk showed that mixed martial arts contests can be analysed using probabilistic forecasting methods rather than only betting odds or opinion. Our project builds from those ideas by testing the smaller question of whether height and reach alone contain a measurable predictive signal.</p>
      </div>
    </article>
  </div>
</section>

<section class="report-chapter dark" id="methodology">
  <header class="chapter-header">
    <div>
      <p class="section-kicker">2. Methodology</p>
      <h2>How the model was built</h2>
    </div>
    <p class="chapter-lede">The model treats each fight as a binary outcome and uses logistic regression to estimate the probability of a blue-corner win.</p>
  </header>

  <div class="subsection-stack">
    <article class="report-subsection" id="data-description">
      <h3>Description of data</h3>
      <div>
        <p>The dataset contains around 6000 UFC fights and 144 columns. It includes fighter names, weight class, height, reach, age, win/loss records, and the winner of each fight, covering fights from 2019 to 2021 across countries including the UK, USA, UAE, Canada, Brazil, and China.</p>
        <p>Although the dataset has 144 columns, the analysis selected 14 variables: <code>R_fighter</code>, <code>B_fighter</code>, <code>weight_class</code>, <code>B_Height_cms</code>, <code>R_Height_cms</code>, <code>B_age</code>, <code>R_age</code>, <code>R_Reach_cms</code>, <code>B_Reach_cms</code>, <code>Winner</code>, <code>R_wins</code>, <code>R_losses</code>, <code>B_wins</code>, and <code>B_losses</code>.</p>
        <p>Rows with missing height, reach, or outcome values were removed. The red-corner wins were then downsampled to match the number of blue-corner wins so the model would not simply learn that red won more often in the original data.</p>
      </div>
    </article>

    <article class="report-subsection" id="assumptions-model">
      <h3>Assumptions and model formulation</h3>
      <div>
        <p>The key assumptions were that height and reach stay consistent across a fighter's career, fights are independent, physical stats are measured accurately, and fight outcomes are simplified to win/loss only. The model also assumes that each extra centimetre of height or reach has a constant effect on the log-odds of winning.</p>
        <p>The outcome was coded as 1 for a blue-corner win and 0 for a red-corner win. Height and reach differentials were measured from the blue fighter's perspective.</p>
        <pre><code>ufc$outcome    &lt;- ifelse(ufc$Winner == "Blue", 1, 0)
ufc$heightdiff &lt;- ufc$B_Height_cms - ufc$R_Height_cms
ufc$reachdiff  &lt;- ufc$B_Reach_cms  - ufc$R_Reach_cms</code></pre>
        <p>The final joint model was:</p>
        <pre><code>glm(outcome ~ heightdiff + reachdiff,
    data = ufc_balanced,
    family = binomial(link = "logit"))</code></pre>
      </div>
    </article>

    <article class="report-subsection" id="modelling-choices">
      <h3>Justification of modelling choices</h3>
      <div>
        <p>A generalised linear model was chosen because the response variable is binary: either the blue fighter wins or the red fighter wins. This makes a Bernoulli/binomial logistic regression appropriate for modelling the probability of a win.</p>
        <p>The Bernoulli idea applies to one fight because there are two outcomes, win or loss. The binomial regression idea applies across many fights because the model learns which predictors are related to repeated binary outcomes.</p>
        <p>This approach is useful because it directly tests whether height and reach advantages influence UFC outcomes, while staying easy to interpret. The trade-off is that the model is deliberately simple and cannot capture all the technical complexity of MMA.</p>
      </div>
    </article>
  </div>
</section>

<section class="report-chapter" id="implementation-results">
  <header class="chapter-header">
    <div>
      <p class="section-kicker">3. Model Implementation and Results</p>
      <h2>Reach mattered. Height faded.</h2>
    </div>
    <p class="chapter-lede">The final results show a small reach signal, weak height evidence, and overall predictive accuracy only slightly above chance.</p>
  </header>

  <div class="subsection-stack">
    <article class="report-subsection" id="implementation">
      <h3>Implementation</h3>
      <div>
        <p>After cleaning missing entries, the project created the binary outcome and the two main predictor differences. A balanced dataset was created by sampling red-corner wins to match blue-corner wins.</p>
        <pre><code>blue_wins &lt;- subset(ufc, outcome == 1)
red_wins  &lt;- subset(ufc, outcome == 0)
red_sample &lt;- red_wins[sample(nrow(red_wins), nrow(blue_wins)), ]
ufc_balanced &lt;- rbind(blue_wins, red_sample)</code></pre>
        <p>Individual Bernoulli logistic models were fitted for height and reach separately, then a joint model was fitted using both predictors together.</p>
      </div>
    </article>

    <article class="report-subsection" id="statistical-summaries">
      <h3>Statistical summaries and visualisations</h3>
      <div>
        <p>Height and reach differential distributions were inspected using histograms, Q-Q plots, and Shapiro-Wilk tests. The plots were roughly centred around zero, but the Shapiro-Wilk tests rejected strict normality, which is common in large datasets where small deviations become statistically significant.</p>
        <p>The individual models suggested both height and reach had some relationship with winning. Height had a coefficient around 0.0116 with p = 0.0274, while reach had a coefficient around 0.0179 with p = 0.0000136.</p>
        <table class="model-table">
          <thead>
            <tr><th>Model check</th><th>Result</th><th>Meaning</th></tr>
          </thead>
          <tbody>
            <tr><td>Height individual model</td><td>p = 0.0274</td><td>Some evidence when tested alone.</td></tr>
            <tr><td>Reach individual model</td><td>p = 0.0000136</td><td>Strong evidence when tested alone.</td></tr>
            <tr><td>Height in joint model</td><td>p = 0.478</td><td>No reliable effect once reach is included.</td></tr>
            <tr><td>Reach in joint model</td><td>p = 0.000132</td><td>Small but reliable positive effect.</td></tr>
          </tbody>
        </table>
        <div class="metric-grid">
          <div class="metric"><strong>0.0203</strong><span>joint reach coefficient</span></div>
          <div class="metric"><strong>0.478</strong><span>height p-value</span></div>
          <div class="metric"><strong>0.6904</strong><span>log-loss</span></div>
        </div>
        <p>The confidence interval for height included 1, meaning the true effect could be positive, negative, or zero. The confidence interval for reach stayed above 1, supporting the conclusion that reach has a small but real effect.</p>
      </div>
    </article>

    <article class="report-subsection" id="visualisations">
      <h3>Project visualisations</h3>
      <div class="figure-row">
        <figure class="figure-card">
          <img src="assets/figures/unnamed-chunk-4-1.png" alt="Height and reach differential distributions">
          <figcaption>Distribution checks for height and reach differentials.</figcaption>
        </figure>
        <figure class="figure-card">
          <img src="assets/figures/unnamed-chunk-8-1.png" alt="Logistic model probability curves">
          <figcaption>Model visualisation showing predictor differential against blue win probability.</figcaption>
        </figure>
      </div>
    </article>

    <article class="report-subsection" id="interpretation">
      <h3>Interpretation in context</h3>
      <div>
        <p>The results show that predicting UFC fight outcomes using only height and reach is not practically reliable. Height had no meaningful effect in the joint model, while reach showed a small but non-negligible influence.</p>
        <p>This makes sense in MMA. Height may help in some situations, but fights also include clinch work, wrestling, ground exchanges, and weight classes that reduce extreme size differences. Reach can help a fighter strike from outside the opponent's range, but this can be neutralised by closing distance, footwork, wrestling, pressure, and superior skill.</p>
        <div class="callout">The model's 52.73% accuracy means physical traits give a signal, but not enough certainty for dependable prediction.</div>
      </div>
    </article>
  </div>
</section>

<section class="report-chapter dark" id="conclusion">
  <header class="chapter-header">
    <div>
      <p class="section-kicker">4. Conclusion</p>
      <h2>Useful signal, weak prediction</h2>
    </div>
    <p class="chapter-lede">Reach is worth noticing, but it should not be treated as a fight-prediction answer by itself.</p>
  </header>

  <div class="conclusion-grid">
    <article class="conclusion-card">
      <h3>Discussion of results</h3>
      <p>Height and reach appeared useful when viewed separately, but in the joint logistic regression model height became statistically insignificant while reach remained significant.</p>
    </article>
    <article class="conclusion-card">
      <h3>Strengths and limitations</h3>
      <p>The study used a large real-world UFC dataset and an interpretable binary model. Its main limitation is that it only used a small set of physical predictors and assumed a linear log-odds relationship.</p>
    </article>
    <article class="conclusion-card">
      <h3>Possible extensions</h3>
      <p>Future models could include age, weight class, win/loss streaks, injuries, weight on fight day, striking accuracy, takedowns, defence, recent activity, nonlinear models, and machine learning methods.</p>
    </article>
    <article class="conclusion-card">
      <h3>Practical implications</h3>
      <p>Reach can be included in more complex fight analysis, but the model is not strong enough for serious betting, coaching, or forecasting decisions on its own.</p>
    </article>
  </div>

  <p>Overall, this project found that physical advantages alone are not strong enough to reliably predict UFC outcomes. Each extra centimetre of reach increased the odds by roughly 2%, but the final accuracy of about 52.7% was only slightly better than a random 50/50 guess.</p>
</section>

<section class="report-chapter" id="contributions">
  <header class="chapter-header">
    <div>
      <p class="section-kicker">5. Contribution statements</p>
      <h2>Project roles</h2>
    </div>
    <p class="chapter-lede">Each group member contributed to writing, modelling, review, or presentation.</p>
  </header>
  <div class="contribution-list">
    <p><strong>P. Lew:</strong> Methodology, writing, review and editing, model implementation and results.</p>
    <p><strong>L. Mondi:</strong> Methodology, writing, introduction and background, conclusion, review and editing, web design.</p>
    <p><strong>E. Yap:</strong> Methodology, writing, model implementation and results, review and editing.</p>
  </div>
</section>

<section class="report-chapter dark" id="appendix">
  <header class="chapter-header">
    <div>
      <p class="section-kicker">6. Appendix</p>
      <h2>Code and supplementary outputs</h2>
    </div>
    <p class="chapter-lede">The appendix keeps the supporting code and extra outputs separate from the marker-facing report flow.</p>
  </header>
  <div class="subsection-stack">
    <article class="report-subsection">
      <h3>Core code</h3>
      <div>
        <pre><code>base &lt;- read.csv("data.csv")
ufc &lt;- base[, c("R_fighter", "B_fighter", "weight_class",
                "B_Height_cms", "R_Height_cms", "B_age", "R_age",
                "R_Reach_cms", "B_Reach_cms", "Winner",
                "R_wins", "R_losses", "B_wins", "B_losses")]
ufc$outcome    &lt;- ifelse(ufc$Winner == "Blue", 1, 0)
ufc$heightdiff &lt;- ufc$B_Height_cms - ufc$R_Height_cms
ufc$reachdiff  &lt;- ufc$B_Reach_cms  - ufc$R_Reach_cms</code></pre>
      </div>
    </article>
    <article class="report-subsection">
      <h3>References</h3>
      <div>
        <ul>
          <li>UFC. (2018). <em>History of UFC</em>. https://www.ufc.com/history-ufc</li>
          <li>Australian Institute of Health and Welfare. (2023). <em>Gambling in Australia</em>. https://www.aihw.gov.au/reports/australias-welfare/gambling</li>
          <li>Walsh, G. <em>Predictive Analysis of UFC Fights</em>.</li>
          <li>Holmes, M., McHale, I., &amp; Zychaluk, K. (2023). Probabilistic forecasting methods for mixed martial arts outcomes.</li>
        </ul>
      </div>
    </article>
  </div>
</section>

<script src="scroll-frames.js"></script>
```
